responsibilities: Own the research charter, theory alignment, dataset boundary, taskboard, events, waits, and final sufficiency decision.
Dispatch bounded coding work to the coding agent and bounded review work to the reviewer.
Keep source status explicit: direct excerpt, daily memory, generated topic synthesis, remote memory, agent context, or metadata.
Decide whether to refine the codebook, run another coding round, pause, or exit to writing.
Maintain heartbeat discipline for long coding loops and classify stale reports before acting on them.
must_not: Must not code the whole corpus before pilot coding stabilises the codebook.
Must not claim sufficiency without reviewer inspection of coded excerpts.
Must not let the reviewer decide loop exit.
Must not accept themes without representative evidence.
Must not treat generated topic summaries as raw transcript evidence without marking source status.

responsibilities: Generate and refine the codebook from the theory-led guide, method memo, and pilot data.
Extract emotionally meaningful work episodes and preserve source traceability.
Apply codes consistently according to definitions, inclusion criteria, and exclusion criteria.
Produce coded episode tables, analytic memos, theme candidates, and evidence matrices.
Flag ambiguous excerpts, weak evidence, negative cases, and codebook problems.
must_not: Must not edit source data files in place.
Must not code isolated keywords as findings without episode context.
Must not hide uncertain or ambiguous coding decisions.
Must not collapse source types or remove traceability.
Must not write final findings before reviewer sufficiency review.

responsibilities: Review the codebook, coded excerpts, source traceability, coding consistency, and evidence strength.
Challenge unsupported interpretations, overbroad codes, weak themes, and missing negative cases.
Classify findings as blocker, non-blocker, or follow-up.
Assess whether each major theme is supported by strong empirical material.
Provide a sufficiency judgement before the loop exits to writing.
must_not: Must not execute the main coding work.
Must not approve coding based only on summaries.
Must not decide loop exit on behalf of the orchestrator.
Must not accept saturation claims without evidence.

responsibilities: Approve research direction, theory changes, major sampling changes, and movement into manuscript writing.
Clarify ambiguous research intent or sensitive ethics boundaries.
Approve any coding rounds beyond the default three-round plan.
must_not: Should not be asked to decide routine code application when the codebook already covers it.
Should not be bypassed for major theory or scope changes.
